data.title: First Guaranty Bancshares: Preferred Yield Is Tempting, But More Improvement Needed
data.description:
Introduction

First Guaranty Bancshares (FGBI) is a Louisiana-based regional bank. Along with common shares, the bank also has a preferred share (FGBIP), which currently yields an impressive 9.5%. The low price and high yield of the preferred share come with a reason, as I highlighted back in October that the bank was facing some loan performance woes. Today, there have been some improvements, but I am still advising that income investors avoid the shares.

Preferred Share Structure

First Guaranty Bancshares’ preferred shares are senior to the bank’s common shares. For management to impair preferred shareholders of their dividends, they would first need to eliminate common dividends. While First Guaranty pays a common dividend, it was cut twice in 2024 and now stands at a fraction of where it used to be.

The preferred shares are non-cumulative, meaning they cannot accrue if eliminated (which is standard for banks). The preferred shares pay a fixed dividend of $0.421875 per quarter. The bank originally issued these shares in April of 2021, and they can be redeemed at $25 per share on April 22nd, but considering their steep discount, it is highly unlikely. I would expect these preferred shares to continue trading until the bank shows substantial improvement and interest rates are lower than current levels.

First Guaranty Financial Results

Management at First Guaranty Bancshares is prioritizing loan stability over loan growth. Throughout 2025, the bank worked to purge loans that were hurting its earnings. As of the end of the fourth quarter, the bank has experienced six consecutive quarters of loan declines. In 2025, the bank drew down its loan balance by an astonishing 23.7%, likely one of the largest loan declines of publicly traded banks.

The change in the bank’s loan composition led to a skew in its results. Asset yields dropped by 39 basis points in the fourth quarter alone and are now 110 basis points lower than their peak at the end of the third quarter of 2024. Unfortunately, borrowing yields fell by nowhere near this amount, dropping by only 18 basis points in the fourth quarter.

The changes in asset and borrowing yields led to a 21-basis point decline in net interest spread to 1.50% in the fourth quarter. This is the lowest level in at least six years. When incorporating leverage, the net interest margin isn’t much better, having dropped by 26 basis points in the fourth quarter to 2.08%, another lowest point in at least six years. The reconfiguring of First Guaranty’s loans is putting overall operating results under pressure.

The compression in yields has translated over to interest income and expense. Interest income declined by $3 million in the fourth quarter to $51 million, which was also $7 million lower than the fourth quarter of 2024. Interest expense remained unchanged and is $4 million lower than in the same quarter a year ago. Ultimately, net interest income (interest income less interest expense) dropped by $3 million in the fourth quarter to $20 million. While many other banks are growing net interest income as rates decline, First Guaranty Bancshares’ net interest income is shrinking.

Deposits: The One Shining Spot

The disruptions associated with the downsizing of the bank’s loan portfolio have had less of an effect on deposits. While deposits did decline in two of the four quarters of 2025, First Guaranty Bancshares experienced a robust fourth quarter of deposit growth at 8.3%. Declines in deposits for the first and third quarters ate into the full-year growth results, but the bank still managed to grow deposits by 4.5% for the full year.

The growth in deposits against the plunge in loans creates a solid growth opportunity for First Guaranty Bancshares. The bank’s loan-to-deposit ratio, which sat at 92% in the second quarter of 2024, has fallen for six consecutive quarters down to 56%. This also may be the biggest drop in loan-to-deposit ratio of any bank in the country and represents one of the lowest ratios in banking.

The drop in the loan-to-deposit ratio means that the bank will have access to cheap capital (deposits) to grow its loans when the time is right. The bank also can reduce its external financing, which currently sits at $186 million. While stable, external financing’s percentage of gross loans has risen due to the dramatic drop in lending. Nevertheless, the large number of deposits backing the loans on the balance sheet is the main reason why I believe the bank will not fail.

The Troubled Loan Risk

Despite the decline in loans, the nonperforming asset risk remains at First Guaranty Bancshares. While the percentage of nonperforming assets to gross loans has fallen to 4.7%, it is still exceptionally high compared to peers and the company’s history. Nonperforming assets are also far above the allowance for credit losses, creating a risk to earnings if these assets need to be disposed of at a loss.

The fact that First Guaranty Bancshares has essentially turned over a quarter of its loan portfolio but has only been able to drop its nonperforming asset ratio by a little more than 100 basis points is very concerning. It tells me that more work needs to be done on the troubled loan side before the bank can be declared stable.

Conclusion

First Guaranty Bancshares is actively managing its troubled loan situation. Unfortunately for income investors who are looking for a safe yield, the bank’s preferred shares don’t offer that level of security yet. Should the bank resume loan growth while simultaneously lowering its nonperforming assets, I would see that as an opportunity to buy preferred shares. Until then, I am remaining with a hold on preferred shares and a sell rating on the common shares.

data.title: WMB Analyst Day: Power And Pipe To Drive Robust Growth
data.description:
By Stacey Morris, CFA

Since its last analyst day in February 2024, some elements of the Williams (WMB) story are very much the same. It still has a track record for solid EBITDA growth, strong project execution and an extensive natural gas pipeline footprint. Other elements of the story are newer, including an exciting power innovations business, expected to contribute significantly to a double-digit growth rate for adjusted EBITDA to 2030. This note discusses key takeaways from Williams’ analyst day last week. Williams is a component of the S&P 500 and a key constituent of Alerian midstream indexes, including the Alerian Midstream Energy Select Index (AMEI).

WMB Raises Long-Term EBITDA Growth Outlook

For years, WMB pointed to a long-term adjusted EBITDA growth target of 5–7%. From 2020 to 2025, the company actually delivered a 9% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) for EBITDA. Notably, this growth was achieved in a more muted backdrop for natural gas than we have today. Specifically, demand growth tailwinds are likely to be more prevalent in the coming years, driven by rising liquefied natural gas (LNG) exports and demand for power generation. WMB highlighted a U.S. natural gas demand growth forecast of 39 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d) to 2035, including 20 Bcf/d for LNG exports and 10 Bcf/d for power.

For 2025 to 2030, management has guided to a 10+% CAGR for adjusted EBITDA. Broadly speaking, the growth is coming from “power and pipe.” Management noted that projects already in execution today can underwrite an 8% CAGR in adjusted EBITDA to 2030. As discussed below, WMB has a significant backlog in both power and pipe beyond sanctioned projects, adding confidence to the growth trajectory to 2030 and beyond. Management plans to continue growing the dividend, with near-term increases likely to remain consistent with the 5% annual growth of recent years.

Specific to 2026, WMB initiated an adjusted EBITDA guidance range of $8.05–$8.25 billion, representing 6% growth relative to 2025 at the midpoint. However, management pointed to 7% growth if normalized for asset sales. WMB also expects to spend $6.4 billion in growth capital this year. Spending will largely be directed to high-return, take-or-pay projects, namely four power projects (~$4 billion for this year) and key transmission projects.

Power: New Project Added, Terms Extended, & a Robust Backlog

Some of the key announcements from the analyst day revolved around Williams’ behind-the-meter power business, which is serving data centers. Notably, the company added Socrates the Younger, a 340-megawatt natural gas power project in Ohio, backed by a 10-year agreement and expected online in 2H28. Additionally, the Aquila and Apollo projects in Utah and Ohio, respectively, were upsized and extended to 12.5-year terms from 10 years. The original Socrates project in Ohio for Meta (META) remains on track for completion later in 2026. WMB is investing over $7 billion in these projects. Management has cited a 5x multiple for power projects, implying 20% returns.

In addition to the four projects already announced (1.9 gigawatts combined), WMB has another 6 gigawatts in its backlog, which can support opportunities into the 2030s. Notably, WMB has secured major equipment for the backlog already. Management explained that their initial advantage in the power space was access to turbines, but as projects are executed, their edge relative to competitors will come from solid execution. Management noted a growing recognition that tailored power solutions will be needed by hyperscalers for the long term.

A potentially underappreciated element of the power business is its eligibility for 100% bonus depreciation, which is not the case for regulated natural gas pipelines. Overall, WMB expects $100 million in cash taxes this year but no cash taxes in 2027.

Pipe: Transmission Backlog Grows

While the power opportunity is new and exciting, the transmission (or long-haul natural gas pipeline) business remains WMB’s most valuable segment, with Transco the crown jewel. As a reminder, Transco stretches from Texas to New York, transporting ~15% of the nation’s natural gas. Currently, WMB has 13 transmission projects underway, representing 7.1 Bcf/d of capacity. WMB is on track to grow its delivery capacity by just over 20% from 2025 to 2030 with these projects, reflecting the significant natural gas demand growth on tap for the coming years.

Additionally, WMB boasts an impressive backlog, with another 14.3 Bcf/d of opportunity representing ~$15 billion in potential capital spend. Over the past year, the backlog has grown by over 5 Bcf/d. That translates to more than $3 billion in terms of capex. The rising backlog again reflects the strength of demand growth. While transmission projects are demand-pull opportunities, management noted that these projects and rising demand will drive more volumes into their gathering & processing (G&P) businesses, particularly in the Haynesville.

So What?

The 10+% CAGR for adjusted EBITDA to 2030 is a key headline from the analyst day. However, it’s also important to note how the business mix is expected to change, as shown below. G&P, which tends to be more sensitive to commodity price moves and production trends, will become a smaller part of the business. Transmission, Power & Gulf will become the majority of the business. By 2030, more than 60% of WMB’s adjusted EBITDA is anticipated to come from long-term, take-or-pay contracts. High-grading the business mix is a key element of the story and can support a stronger valuation over time given transmission trades at higher multiples than G&P businesses.

data.title: Voya Target Retirement Funds Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

The Voya Target Retirement Fund Series is designed to specifically balance the evolving risk-return profiles of participants as they age to maximize the probability of a successful retirement.

Key takeaways

  • Global markets ended the year strong despite volatility from the U.S. government shutdown, softening job growth, and elevated valuation concerns. Foreign equities outperformed, while fixed income delivered positive but muted returns.
  • Equity fundamental factors remain supported by steady economic growth, easing inflation, and ongoing investment in productivity-enhancing technologies, while solid corporate balance sheets and gradually improving financial conditions create a constructive backdrop for earnings and risk assets.
  • The Funds posted positive absolute and relative returns for the period, outperforming their strategic allocation benchmarks on a gross- and net-of-fees basis.

Market review

Global markets ended the year strongly despite disruptions. The U.S. government shutdown weighed on sentiment, adding volatility amid slowing job growth and concerns over elevated artificial intelligence (AI) valuations. Supportive earnings and central bank accommodation sustained risk appetite. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued easing, cutting rates 25 basis points in October and December, while expectations for further cuts fluctuated.

U.S. equities posted modest gains, though leadership shifted. Large cap value outperformed growth, reflecting rotation away from concentrated technology exposures, while defensive sectors such as health care led amid valuation concerns. Small cap stocks also rose but lagged large caps.

International equities outperformed on stronger earnings momentum and more attractive valuations. Japan benefited from renewed political clarity under Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, reinforcing reform expectations. Emerging markets (EM) were mixed; some regions gained from global growth and AI investment, while China lagged due to ongoing property weakness and deflation.

An investor should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund(s) carefully before investing. For a free copy of the Fund's prospectus or summary prospectus, which contains this and other information, visit us at www.voyainvestments.com or call (800) 992-0180. Please read all materials carefully before investing.

Fixed income delivered positive returns but trailed equities. Core bonds benefited from rate cuts, while high yield (HY) credit remained a bright spot, supported by solid fundamental factors and low default risk.

Outlook

The global economy enters 2026 with moderate yet resilient growth. The United States remains the leader thanks to strong capital investment supported by full expensing, onshoring, and transformative spending on AI and automation. Productivity gains should help offset demographic pressures while consumption should stay solid. Inflation in the U.S. is gradually declining, aided by technological effects and lower housing costs, allowing the Fed to continue a gentle easing cycle. The labor market is showing signs of cooling but should remain stable, which supports a soft-landing scenario.

U.S. equities should benefit from solid growth, lower inflation, and fiscal as well as monetary impulses. Large companies remain the core of portfolios with exposure to AI, automation, and digital infrastructure. Valuations are high but look moderate when adjusted for record margins. We favor a balanced approach between growth and value. Smaller cap companies' earnings prospects are improving as financial conditions ease, but these segments face headwinds from higher input costs and less diversified revenue streams.

Globally, we maintain diversification. Europe faces structural challenges and slower growth although selected sectors, such as industrials and luxury goods, remain attractive. The United Kingdom faces a tougher environment with limited consumption and muted equity prospects. Japan stands out for stability, corporate governance reforms, and automation trends.

EM show mixed dynamics. China remains under pressure from real estate weakness and soft consumption yet cost advantages and export leadership support long-term potential. India continues as a key structural growth story while Latin America offers selective opportunities in commodities and nearshoring.

Fixed income fundamental factors for investment grade remain stable as issuers continue to show solid credit profiles, supported by steady revenue and earnings growth outside commodity sectors. While rating downgrades have recently outpaced upgrades, most large borrowers—particularly those investing heavily in AI—retain

strong balance sheets and robust cash flow, limiting near-term credit stress. In HY, defaults remain near historical lows, and issuers generally exhibit healthy liquidity and balance sheet strength, though refinancing activity and tighter spreads warrant careful credit selection. The U.S. dollar is expected to weaken gradually due to Fed easing, overvaluation, and improving global growth.

Positioning

At the beginning of the period, Funds held modest tactical equity overweights and fixed income underweights relative to their strategic allocation benchmarks. Funds were also tactically overweight to U.S. large cap, international developed and U.S. long term Treasury bonds. Funds were underweight to U.S. mid cap equities and core fixed income. During the period, international and EM equities were increased while U.S. equities and bonds were reduced to capture more attractive relative valuations abroad and mitigate concentration risk after a period of strong U.S. performance. European markets offer potential catalysts, including fiscal stimulus and easing energy pressures, while a softer dollar and a dovish Fed stance should support EM assets. These changes also increased exposure beyond U.S. large-cap AI themes and optimize EM positioning across vintages. Also, a modest position in an S&P 500 Equal Weight exposure was added and the position in traditional S&P 500 was reduced to mitigate concentration risk in mega-cap stocks and improve diversification. This adjustment increases exposure to broader market participation as breadth improves and positions the portfolio for potential mean reversion, should leadership expand beyond the largest constituents.

Overall, Funds hold a balanced posture between equity and fixed income, with overweights to U.S. large cap, U.S. mid cap, international developed equity and core fixed income, and underweights to real estate investment trusts (REIT), U.S. small cap and international bonds.

There were no manager changes during the period.

Performance

The Voya Target Retirement Fund's primary performance objective is to outperform its strategic allocation composite benchmark over the long-term through tactical asset allocation, i.e., deviating from the composite benchmark over the short and medium-term and active manager selection. The benchmark return is the weighted average return of indices that represent asset classes included in the strategic allocation benchmark. Index returns are gross of all fees. The Funds are generally rebalanced monthly and the strategic asset allocations are updated annually to reflect changes to our capital market assumptions. In the fourth quarter of 2025, Funds outperformed their strategic allocation benchmarks on a gross- and net-of-fees basis. Tactical asset allocation and manager selection both were contributors.

Tactical asset allocation had a positive impact on performance during the period. Funds' tactical underweight to midcap and overweight to international developed equities were the main contributors. Mid-cap performance lagged largely because the segment had only modest participation in AI-related market leadership, faced softer earnings trends, and remained concentrated in sectors like industrials and consumer staples, which offered limited catalysts during the period. Cheaper starting valuations and improving economic conditions in international developed markets helped boost returns relative to the U.S. A tactical overweight to duration was the biggest detractor as the U.S. Treasury yield curve steepened with the long end rising modestly.

Underlying managers' relative results were positive across the Funds. The biggest contributors in the quarter were, Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets ETF, Voya VACS Series EME and iShares Core S&P Mid Cap ETF. Active strategies that detracted most to excess returns in the quarter were Vanguard FTSE Emerging Markets ETF, Voya Multi-Manager International Equity and Voya MI Dynamic Small Cap.

Index returns do not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. You could lose money on your investment and any of the following risks, among others, could affect investment performance. The following principal risks are presented in alphabetical order which does not imply order of importance or likelihood: Affiliated Underlying Funds; Asset Allocation; Bank Instruments; Cash/ Cash Equivalents; Commodities; Company; Credit; Credit Default Swaps; Currency; Deflation; Derivative Instruments; Environmental, Social, and Governance (Funds-of-Funds); Floating Rate Loans; Foreign (Non-U.S.) Investments/ Developing and Emerging Markets; Growth Investing; High-Yield Securities; Index Strategy (Funds-of-Funds); Inflation-Indexed Bonds; Interest Rate; Liquidity; Market; Market Capitalization; Market Disruption and Geopolitical; Natural Resources/ Commodity Securities; Portfolio Turnover; Prepayment and Extension; Real Estate Companies and Real Estate Investment Trusts; U.S. Government Securities and Obligations; Underlying Funds; Value Investing. The "target date" is the approximate date when an investor plans to start withdrawing their money. When their target date is reached, they may have more or less than the original amount invested. For each target-date portfolio, until the day prior to its target date, the portfolio will seek to provide total returns consistent with an asset allocation targeted for an investor who is retiring in approximately each portfolio's designated target year. On the target date, the portfolio will seek to provide a combination of total return and stability of principal. There is no guarantee that any investment option will achieve its stated objective. Principal value fluctuates and there is no guarantee of value at any time, including the target date. Important factors to consider when planning for retirement include your expected expenses, sources of income, and available assets. Before investing in the Voya Target Retirement Fund, weigh your objectives, time horizon, and risk tolerance. Diversification cannot assure a profit or protect against loss in a declining market. Investors should consult the Fund's Prospectus and Statement of Additional Information for a more detailed discussion of the Fund's risks.

The Fund discussed may be available to you as part of your employer sponsored retirement plan. There may be additional plan level fees resulting in personal performance to vary from stated performance. Please call your benefits office for more information.

This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Nothing contained herein should be construed as (i) an offer to sell or solicitation of an offer to buy any security or (ii) a recommendation as to the advisability of investing in, purchasing or selling any security. Any opinions expressed herein reflect our judgment and are subject to change. Certain of the statements contained herein are statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on management's current views and assumptions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results, performance or events to differ materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. Actual results, performance or events may differ materially from those in such statements due to, without limitation, (1) general economic conditions, (2) performance of financial markets, (3) interest rate levels, (4) increasing levels of loan defaults (5) changes in laws and regulations and (6) changes in the policies of governments and/or regulatory authorities.

The opinions, views and information expressed in this commentary regarding holdings are subject to change without notice. The information provided regarding holdings is not a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Portfolio holdings are fluid and are subject to daily change based on market conditions and other factors. Past Performance does not guarantee future results

data.title: Hims & Hers Health: 5 Key Questions Ahead Of Critical Q4 Earnings
data.description:
Investment Overview: A Quick History Of Hims & Hers' Attempts To Sell Compounded GLP-1 Drugs

Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (HIMS) is set to report its Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings on February 23rd - this coming Monday - and it feels like a critical moment in the development of a company that listed on the NYSE via a ~$1.6bn Special Purpose Acquisition Company ("SPAC") merger in 2021.

Hims stock began trading at a price of $10 per share, and in January 2024, traded ~$8.5 per share. In May 2024, however, Hims' share price began to climb and climb, as the telehealth platform, or "consumer-first platform transforming the way customers fulfill their health and wellness needs" (source: Q3 2025 10-Q filing) provider announced it would make a "compounded" version of the injectable Glucagon Like Peptide-1 receptor agonist ("GLP-1 RA") semaglutide available via its platform.

Hims was taking advantage of the fact that Danish Pharma giant Novo Nordisk's (NVO) semaglutide - marketed and sold as Ozempic to treat Type 2 Diabetes ("T2D") and as Wegovy to treat Obesity - had been added to the Food and Drug Administration's ("FDA") global drug shortage list, and in such a scenario, a compounded version of the drug molecule - a non FDA approved version (not necessarily tested for safety, effectiveness or quality) is permitted to be sold in its place.

Wegovy / Ozempic earned ~$25bn of revenues in 2024 (and ~$31.5bn in 2025), but with Hims offering a compounded version at a lower price, its stock began to soar in value, reaching ~$45 per share in February last year.

The FDA soon removed semaglutide, and tirzepatide, a GLP-1 RA marketed and sold by Eli Lilly (LLY) as Mounjaro (for T2D) and Zepbound (for obesity), from the shortage list, but Hims turned to a second loophole - "personalization," i.e., offering dose sizes that Novo / Lilly did not offer, another situation in which compounded drug products may be sold.

As Hims' CEO Andrew Dudum told analysts on its Q3 2025 earnings call:

Put simply, we're making it easier for more customers to start, stay on and succeed in their own weight loss journey, and we're confident that we'll continue to drive results for a growing set of customers by doing so

Shares reached a high of ~$66 in July 2025, but then the tide seemingly began to turn against the company. In early November, both Lilly and Novo agreed deals with the Trump administration to slash the costs of their weight loss drugs, from >$1,000 per month, to ~$350 per month, making it tougher for Hims to compete on pricing.

Hims' Oral Wegovy Woes

Hims' relationship with Novo has been particularly frosty - Novo agreed to allow Hims to market and sell Wegovy on its platform in April last year, but quickly terminated the agreement in June, noting that:

Over one month into the collaboration, Hims & Hers Health, Inc. has failed to adhere to the law which prohibits mass sales of compounded drugs under the false guise of "personalization" and are disseminating deceptive marketing that put patient safety at risk.

On its Q3 2025 earnings call, Hims suggested it was back in talks with Novo to market and sell the oral version of Wegovy which was approved in December last year, but instead, Hims attempted to launch its own compounded version of oral semaglutide, stating in a press release on February 5th:

As always, safety is our top priority. The Compounded Semaglutide Pill joins a wide range of other weight loss treatments accessible through our platform, all of which meet rigorous clinical standards. We adhere to all federal and state standards for compounding, and all active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) in compounded treatments are sourced exclusively from FDA-registered facilities.

On 7th February, however, facing mounting pressure and the threat of a Department of Justice ("DOJ") investigation, Hims opted to drop its new pill, citing “constructive conversations with stakeholders across the industry.” On 9th February, the media reported that Novo was taking Hims to court, asking for it to be permanently banned from marketing and selling compounded versions of its patent protected drugs.

Novo is selling its oral pill at a price of ~$149 for the first month, and $199 thereafter, while Hims website still states (at the time of writing) that it would offer "introductory plans starting at just $49 for the first month* with a 5-month plan."

Over the past 6 months, Hims stock has fallen in value by >60%, and across the past month, by >45%, presently trading at ~$16 per share, with a market cap valuation of $3.7bn.

Given the volatility of Hims stock over the past 18 months, it is arguably hard to say whether the current share price represents a strong buying opportunity - given HIMS has been steadfast in its belief that the company will drive $5.5bn in revenues in 2030, and deliver $1.3bn in adjusted EBITDA - or whether potentially being without the pull of a GLP-1 offering to attract customers to its site and new subscribers, a further downward price correction may be in store when results and guidance are shared next week.

To try to help readers reach a decision, in this post I'll consider the five questions we should be asking ourselves ahead of a pivotal set of earnings.

How Damaging To Hims Present & Future Earnings Would The Loss Of Compounded Semaglutide Be?

Hims reported its Q3 earnings on November 3rd last year as follows:

  • Revenue of nearly $600 million, up 49% year-over-year in Q3 2025.
  • Net income of nearly $16 million; Adjusted EBITDA of over $78 million in Q3 2025.
  • Subscribers grew to almost 2.5 million, up 21% year-over-year in Q3 2025.

The revenues are certainly impressive, the margins less so, perhaps, and Hims has warned about a "temporary pause in year-over-year margin expansion" in 2026, as the company ramps up investment, particularly overseas. Hims says (in its latest 10-Q filing) that:

The majority of our Online Revenue is subscription-based, where customers agree to be billed on a recurring basis to have products and services automatically delivered to them. Online Revenue also includes sales from customers who have made one-time purchases.

Hims also shared the following Q4 and full-year 2025 guidance:

For the fourth quarter 2025, we expect:

  • Revenue of $605 million to $625 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $55 million to $65 million, reflecting an Adjusted EBITDA margin of 9% to 10%.

For the full year 2025, we expect:

  • Revenue of $2.335 billion to $2.355 billion.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $307 million to $317 million, reflecting an Adjusted EBITDA margin of 13%.

When asked by an analyst on its Q3 earnings call if its weight loss business was still on track to earn "$725m or better" in 2025, Hims' Chief Financial Officer responded that "we are on pace to achieve the $725 million or greater target that we put forth earlier this year."

Hims sells weight loss pills as well as compounded GLP-1, so we don't know precisely how much the compounded business is worth to the company, although it has been speculated that the figure was ~$200m in 2024.

Given that Hims is a >$2.3bn revenue per annum company - in 2025 at least - it does seem as though the company could shrug off the loss of a ~$200m per annum business, although it may be the case that HIMS could lose subscribers who came to the platform for compounded semaglutide only, and attract fewer subscribers going forward.

It's possible that, announcing earnings next Monday, Hims may be forced into an embarrassing climbdown re its long-term revenue projections, and that the company provides disappointing guidance for 2026, perhaps even signaling a trough year (revenues fall year-on-year) on account of its GLP-1 issues.

As such, 2026 guidance, which Hims declined to provide "specific color" around on its last earnings call, will be a very closely watched figure.

The company has suggested that its Hers business will generate >$1bn revenues in 2025, however, and objectively speaking, a company that trades at a price to sales ratio of ~1.6x, and a price to operating income ratio of ~12x, that claimed to have "over $1.1 billion of cash, short-term and long-term investments" as of the end of Q3, and long-term, debt of <$1bn, would be considered undervalued, in my view.

Therefore, although it's hard to quantify exactly what kind of impact the loss of its compounded GLP-1 business would have, theoretically, at least, Hims would present a reasonably strong case for investment without it.

Will Hims Continue To Offer Compounded Semaglutide In 2026

This is billion dollar question, and a difficult one to answer. Seeking Alpha recently reported comments from Former FDA Commissioner Scott Gottlieb as follows:

I don’t think drug companies are going to have confidence that Hims and Hers is suddenly going to become very compliant,” he said, adding that the company is not “perceived in the industry as the sort of good actor in this space.”

Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly (LLY) both have their direct-to-consumer ("DTC") sales platforms, and on February 6th, the governments' DTC platform TrumpRx was officially launched, so there are plenty of outlets through which GLP-1 products can be sold.

Seeking Alpha also reported:

Hims & Hers called the patent lawsuit filed by Novo Nordisk over IP rights linked to the Danish drugmaker’s weight loss therapy, semaglutide, “a blatant attack” targeting “millions of Americans who rely on compounded medications for access to personalized care.”

There is certainly a school of thought that Hims has been "getting away with it" by exploiting compounded drug rule loopholes, and that they were never intended to be interpreted in this way, but will anybody step in and make it outright illegal for Hims to supply compounded GLP in this way?

The FDA and pharmas have frequently warned of the dangers of buying compounded drugs online - current FDA commissioner Dr Marty Makary has been quoted as saying the agency will "take swift action against companies mass-marketing illegal copycat drugs, claiming they are similar to FDA-approved products," while Novo's CEO has suggested that Hims oral semaglutide "simply doesn't work" because it lacks certain manufacturing elements used by Novo itself.

In my view, Hims ability to keep selling compounded semaglutide of any kind looks as though it may well be compromised in 2026, although I arrived at a similar conclusion when the molecule came off the FDA's drug shortage list, and Hims was able to strategise a new way to offer it, so I would certainly not be surprised if the company finds a way.

It certainly feels as though, if Hims does not address the matter itself on its earnings call next week, analysts will be pressing management for an answer on its GLP strategy going forward, and Hims may well need to pull the proverbial rabbit out of a hat, or face another stock sell-off, especially if forward guidance and the long term revenue targets are readjusted.

Does Hims Have Other Offerings That Can Enable Management To Keep The Business Growing?

It's important to stress that Hims entire business is not selling compounded GLP-1. Far from it. The company has lofty ambitions for its platform and offers products covering sexual health, dermatology, mental health, hormone health and longevity, besides weight loss - with weight loss not being restricted to semaglutide alone.

The idea is that Hims' data driven platform can leverage AI capabilities to learn about each subscriber's specific health needs. Amongst other things, Hims has recently made a strategic investment in Grail (GRAL), the multi-cancer early detection test provider, whose share price is up >200% on a 6-month basis, struck a partnership with Marius Pharmaceuticals, which allows customers to access "one of the few FDA-approved oral testosterone treatments in 2026," and introduced perimenopause and menopause offerings (a 1.3m patient market, the company says).

Looking ahead, Hims said on its Q3 '25 earnings call:

We plan to launch comprehensive whole body lab testing before year-end, which will represent a significant step forward in this evolution. By scaling capabilities like blood testing, we're thrilled to help more customers affordably access the insights they need to monitor, maintain and improve their overall health.

We envision these capabilities empowering consumers to proactively address health concerns such as vitamin deficiencies, unoptimized hormonal levels and genetic risk factors while also identifying early indicators of more serious conditions like cancer and cardiovascular disease.

Historically, people have come to us to manage existing conditions. Soon, we'll help them take a more proactive role in managing their health and in doing so, can potentially impact millions of lives.

Granted, some of these ideas may feel far-fetched - will consumers be ready to pay for preventative care, i.e., to ward off illnesses they do not yet have (and may never have) - but we can certainly make the case that Hims can meet its $5.5bn revenue and $1.3bn adjusted EBITDA targets for 2030, without marketing and selling any GLP-1 drugs.

Can Hims Work With Other GLP-1 Manufacturers Going Forward?

This is another important question for readers to consider. Hims may have burned its bridges with Novo (on more than one occasion) but Novo and Lilly's duopolistic control of GLP-1 markets - the duo earned ~$75bn combined from semglutide / tirzepatide last year - could soon be under threat.

Pharma giants Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) and Amgen (AMGN) are progressing GLP-1 assets into late stage studies, while the likes of Merck (MRK), AstraZeneca (AZN), AbbVie (ABBV) and Pfizer (PFE) all have GLP-1 drugs in development. Biotechs Viking Therapeutics (VKTX), Zealand Pharma, Altimmune (ALT), Septerna (SEPN), and Structure Therapeutics (GPCR) all have mid-to-late stage programs ongoing also.

It's possible then that we could witness multiple new drug approvals before 2030 for GLP-1 drugs, which will compete on various grounds - efficacy, price, lean muscle mass preservation, combinations, alternative targets e.g. amylin, long-acting vs short acting, oral vs injectable etc.

My guess is that, particularly the smaller biotechs might be interested in partnering with Hims, which has established a reputation as something of a "people's champion," a David taking on the Goliath that is Big Pharma, and putting customers first.

Slick marketing, a smooth platform, and opportunities to cross market and bundle products could help Hims and a new GLP-1 partner take the fight to Lilly and Novo, in my view, in the future.

Could Hims Work With Novo Nordisk Or Eli Lilly Again?

If you can't beat them, join them? Novo has previously shown it would be willing to work with Hims if only Hims would sell its product only, and not a compounded version.

Presumably, compounded semaglutide is a more lucrative product to market and sell than Novo branded semaglutide, but when it comes down to it, Hims' platform does represent another outlet for drug companies to sell their wares through, one that pays for its own marketing and may attract a particular type of customer Pharmas and government cannot reach via their own DTC channels.

It would certainly be intriguing if, for example, Hims announced it has signed a deal with Eli Lilly to sell Zepbound and Mounjaro on Monday, and would likely send its stock soaring in value, although for many reasons, I see such an announcement as very unlikely.

As mentioned, however, Hims has proven adept at putting itself front and center of an emerging weight loss industry that could become the most lucrative in pharmaceutical history, which is no mean achievement, and it has also been able - until this year, at least - to lead two of the world's largest Pharma companies, and the FDA, on a merry dance with its interpretations of compounded drug policy, for instance.

As such, come next Monday, I am personally expecting the unexpected. I would not expect Hims to humbly accept the latest reversals in its fortunes without putting up a fight, and would not be in the least surprised if management unveils a new strategy re GLP-1 drugs, however my gut feeling is that the compounded GLP-1 business will likely be shut down in 2026.

As also discussed, however, Hims is more than a compounded weight loss drug business. While we must acknowledged that telehealth has not taken off in the way analysts believed it might during the pandemic, when it looked like it may become the norm, rather than the exception - witness the decline in the share price of Teladoc (TDOC), for example - there are clear and obvious reasons why the public will keep using DTC telehealth platforms, a fact which Big Pharma is now acknowledging.

Hims may be down as we head towards the most critical set of earnings and guidance in its history, in my view, but it is certainly not out. Because I am expecting the unexpected, I have to issue a Hold rating on stock at this time, but I can state with some confidence that, in my view, the volatility in Hims stock price is far from over.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bloom Energy: The Foundational Infrastructure Trade For AI
data.description:
The Foundational Infrastructure Trade

For the last 5 years, the electricity consumption from data centres has been growing at a CAGR of 15%, according to the IEA's study, and that CAGR is very likely to accelerate from here because AI is rapidly moving towards inference, which is projected to consume up to 165-326 terawatt-hours annually by 2028, according to Tensormesh data. To put it in perspective, that energy generation is enough to power ~22% of all US households to date. Because of this structural shift in energy consumption and the rising role of DCs in the mix, anything that can reliably supply energy to DCs is in high demand, and ordinary investors are constantly looking for publicly available picks that add some exposure to this heating energy/tech niche.

I believe that Bloom Energy (BE) is an interesting pick in this industry as it's focusing on on-site, behind-the-meter solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC) systems that can be deployed in as little as 90 days - it means hyperscalers can build out DCs relatively quickly and power them with no grid usage. The BE stock trades at a huge premium - at least it seems to be so when we take a look at the FWD P/E figure, for example (at ~100x).

However, I think the firm's prospective 2-3 years forward earnings are compensating for the current premium. In fact, I see some unique catalysts for BE ahead that might lead to out-of-consensus growth amid the company's competitive edge in its tech. The recent earnings print has shown us that Bloom Energy can deliver above its own guidance. I think the stock price is very likely to adjust higher from here as the current execution level holds, so I decided to give BE a "Buy" rating.

Why Do I Think So?

First off, let's figure out Bloom's specifics. Bloom Energy focuses on solid-state digital power that utilizes a solid-oxide fuel cell, which converts fuel directly into electricity through an electrochemical process without combustion or moving parts. The word "digital" here means that they run a specialized software-driven platform for that energy generation, which allows the whole solid-state architecture to adjust electrical output in milliseconds - this sounds like an ideal solution for AI compute environments, which are usually quite volatile and require rapid load-following.

At the core, Bloom is a technology innovator that rapidly delivers cost competitive platform products at meaningful scale to satisfy customers' current and future needs. We are building solid-state digital power for the digital age. We are not an industrial era energy company. Bloom's manufacturing IP and supply chain diversity enable us to scale without facing the multiyear delivery backlogs plaguing traditional suppliers. Our ability to scale also comes with a high ROI and low-risk profile. Capacity expansion requires a significantly lower upfront investment, a fraction of what legacy players need.

Source: Bloom's CEO commentary at the Q4 earnings call

They also run two Remote Monitoring and Control Centers (RMCC), which process more than 6 billion data points daily for better control of every fuel cell stack in the field. These predictive analytics capabilities add a lot of value for potential DC customers, in my opinion, as they can resolve performance issues before failure occurs and avoid costly consequences that might have occurred otherwise.

Bloom builds its systems on-site, thus requiring no interconnection queues for the grids, and as I noted above, this feature helps its customers deploy and energize a well-functioning system in as little as 90 days. In fact, it can be done even faster - the timeline recently reached "a record 55 days for an Oracle (ORCL) AI factory order", per management's comments.

We will deliver our power platform faster than you can build your greenfield facilities, be it an AI factory or a C&I facility. We demonstrated this recently by delivering a hyperscale AI factory order in 55 days against a 90-day commitment and power for a large factory before they could complete construction and commence operation. That is quick time to power, the Bloom way.

Another massive advantage that Bloom has, and which isn't what legacy thermoelectric plants can brag about, is no water usage. The old energy generation plants, such as coal, gas turbines, and nuclear, need a lot of water for cooling - based on USGS data, these plants use ~41% of all freshwater in the U.S. The risks of water scarcity are rising every year. At the same time, Thanks to its electrochemical process, Bloom Energy needs zero water cooling during steady-state operation, so its systems can be used on the Western/Southern parts of the U.S., for example, where the risks of scarcity are the highest.

And all these bonuses that BE has for stable energy generation are already flowing to its financials. In BE's Q4 2025 report, which came out less than 2 weeks ago, we see that the firm's revenues increased by 35.9% YoY, and the non-GAAP EPS increased by ~4.6% YoY, letting the company beat the consensus estimates by 20.5% and 50.38%, respectively. What I found more interesting was the backlog's trend - both product and service backlogs increased dramatically in FY2025 compared to FY2024:

The management guided for a >50% YoY rise in revenues and an approximately 2x increase in EPS for FY2026, driven by rising demand for their products and services from hyperscalers and data centers.

We know that Bloom Energy is the only provider that natively produces 800V DC today. Most modern AI racks run on low-voltage DC power, yet the grid delivers high-voltage AC - that's why power is converted multiple times (AC to DC, high to low voltage), and it leads to 2-5% loss at every step. For huge hyperscalers, even these low percentages in lost efficiency mean billions of dollars - and we have to add additional spending on GPUs, transformers, space, etc. So, Bloom stands out as the best alternative that can save hyperscalers' investors money - I expect more backlog expansion for Bloom Energy's portfolio ahead, as most of its customers try to further maximize their ROICs on their huge AI CAPEX.

As a result, the management's revenue guidance for FY2026 might turn out to be quite conservative in nature. And what's interesting here - the analysts covering the stock price in a discount on top of BE's midpoint, expecting $3.18 billion vs. $3.2 billion.

The same things are seen in the EPS consensus. Based on the management's EPS guidance, the midpoint should be at ~$1.405. The market's consensus expects only $1.39:

That's a small difference, but we should recall that Bloom has managed to outpace its own internal high-end guidance for FY2025 in terms of both EPS and revenues. The management had a revenue range of $1.65-1.85 billion for FY2025, but the actual result came in at $2.02 billion. The level of beat on the bottom line of over 34%, based on Seeking Alpha, is also telling us that the previous guidance was set too low. On the bottom line in particular, I like the management's projections that the GP margins will soar to ~32% in FY2026 (from 30% in FY2025), while the OPEX-as-of-sales will drop to 15% (vs. 19% last year) - I believe this should create a massive operating leverage and boost BE's EPS well above what's priced in to date.

Let's touch upon the stock's valuation setup. I understand that after BE's rally of over 465% on a YoY basis, the stock looks overbought - especially so when we take a look at its valuation profile on Seeking Alpha:

But the forward multiples we see above are calculated based on FY2026 consensus figures. That's slightly off for analyzing a growth stock, in my view. I think we should take a look at least at FY2027, and ideally beyond that. For FY2027, BE is projected to achieve an EPS of $2.90, implying a P/E ratio of ~50x. That's already much better - a multiple contraction of ~2x. But what if Bloom manages to sign more contracts in its backlog and the deployment speed stays relatively low, allowing them to continue recognizing revenue as fast as they did in 2H 2025? I think they can beat the current FY2027 revenue projection by at least 20% - this would bring BE ~$5.88 billion in sales for that year. Effectively, it's a P/S of ~6.9x. But for the level of growth Bloom has, and for the importance of the entire niche it represents, I believe the multiple should be in the range of 8-10x. Based on this assumption, BE turns out to be ~29% undervalued on the forward basis. Let that be my 12-16 months target for the stock.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

We should keep in mind that Bloom Energy is still a relatively small business that faces structural risks if the management's execution turns out to be insufficient. In other words, the level of backlog growth is good, but it doesn't mean the firm will be able to execute efficiently and translate it into real revenues over time.

The business is already valued at >$40 billion in terms of market cap - that's a lot for a firm that generates only slightly more than $2 billion on a TTM basis at ~30% GP margin. I put a premium on its valuation for the forward EPS and revenue growth superiority, but I may be wrong in these assumptions. If BE fails to sustain a relatively expensive valuation multiple in the medium term for whatever reason, the stock price is likely to drop heavily under the weight of today's excessive expectations. Probably those analysts who formed their price targets for BE in the base and bear cases are correct, and there's nothing left in the stock's price action after the bullish rally of the past weeks.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks, I think that BE looks like a solid pick in the medium term, thanks to its unique market standing because of the specifics of its tech, and the fact that Bloom is the only provider that natively produces 800V DC today. I think that throughout FY2026, we'll see guidance upgrades from management as the firm enjoys an inflow of new customers (or new orders from existing ones), or massive beats to the currently conservative consensus estimates. Based on my math, I see an upside potential of ~29% if BE holds its valuation premium in place - that's why I rate it as a "Buy."

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Strategy's High-Yielding Preferred Children -- Two Buys, Two Sells
data.description:
Don’t think of buy-and-hold (or as some say, hoarding) bitcoin treasury company Strategy (MSTR) as a stock. See it instead as a family.

On 2/13/26, I gave MSTR, the parent, a “Sell” rating.

I didn’t do that because I hate bitcoin. I don’t. (See here and here.)

Instead, I expressed my discomfort with the aggressive and complex way its once-intriguing (to me) intelligent leverage program evolved. Skipping the details (which you can read here), it’s status as bitcoin amplified has become bitcoin too-amplified.

But that doesn’t mean I have to spurn all of its offspring.

I can accept, like, or love, certain products. But at the same time, I can hate shares of the company that makes or offers them.

For example, I am and have long been a customer of Citigroup (C) and Charles Schwab (SCHW). But I’ve never for a nano-second considered owning either equity.

Both, along with much of their respective industries, are on my personal no-no list.

  • I despise bank stocks because I, as a non-insider public shareholder, don’t have as much information as I’d need to properly judge the quality of C’s loan portfolio or the efficacy of its approach to designating and reserving for questionable loans.
  • I have similar issues with SCHW. How can I credibly forecast trading activities and SCHW’s approach to investing its own assets?

I face a comparable situation with family-MSTR.

In contrast to banks or brokerage companies, I believe I can reasonably evaluate bitcoin, the foundation of the house in which this family lives. My concern here is risk.

I can handle volatility — to a point.

As explained on 2/13/26, MSTR involves too darn much of it.

But one of MSTR’s children, the Strategy Inc 8.00% SERIES A PERPETUAL STRIKE PFD (STRK), nicknamed “Strike,” mutes the extremes of MSTR common.

Consistent with CEO Michael Saylor’s MSTR vision, it offers exposure to bitcoin but with gains and declines amplified. But the STRK’s moves, both ways, turn out to be what I regard as more tolerable degrees.

Meanwhile, the Strategy Inc 9.0% SERIES A PERPETUAL STRETCH PREF STK (STRC), nicknamed “Stretch,” looks to be an intriguing gem.

MSTR refers to it as a treasury credit. I think of it as the favorite child — MSTR’s favorite, and mine.

I’m rating STRK and STRC as “Buy,” albeit for very different reasons.

I’m less intrigued by the other two kids…

The Strategy Inc 10.00% SER A PERPETUAL STRIFE PFD STK (STRF), nicknamed “Strife,” is meh. It occupies something of a middle ground between STRK and STRC.

And for me, the non-cumulative (see below) nature of the Perpetual Stride Preferred Stock (STRD), nicknamed “Stride,” is an automatic turnoff.

Not seeing a reason to own STRF or STRD, I rate both “Sell.”

NOTE: I usually rate stocks based on how I expect them to perform relative to the market. But because family MSTR differs from ordinary common stocks or ETFs, I alter my approach. My buy or sell ratings for each reflects my view on whether or not it should be owned.

Finally, as is the case with many families, the MSTR kids have a distant cousin. It’s a Euro-denominated security. Its informal ticker is STRE. But it trades only in Luxembourg. And it’s not part of Seeking Alpha’s database. Accordingly, I won’t rate or cover it.

As noted above, I reviewed MSTR on 2/13/26. This report will address the children.

Let’s start with…

A Group Overview

Here, for your convenience, are re-posts of two images I published on 2/13/26.

Here, first, is a timeline of the MSTR family growth (the children’s birth order):

And here is a summary of the characteristics of each child:

There are two important points I want to make about the table.

First, I want to remind you about BTC Rating. As I said on 2/13/26:

BTC rating, an MSTR-created measure of the extent to which each security is (informally) collateralized by MSTR’s bitcoin holdings, is explained … in small-font text on page 88 of the latest earnings deck.

Treat it as a very broad approximation, something interesting to know, but less firm than a stock’s interest coverage or dividend payout ratio.

Note, too, that I ignore the Tax-Equivalent Yields.

For starters, this is not tax avoidance. It’s tax deferral.

In other words, the so-called “return on capital” distribution is not immediately taxable as income, but it does reduce one’s “basis.” In other words, it reduces the amount of the purchase price. So if you hold any of these in taxable accounts, these reductions will increase the taxable capital gain you’d pay when you sell.

Meanwhile…

Tax impact is ultimately an individual matter with advice best given by one’s own tax professional. So as a matter of analytic approach, I won’t give recommendations on this. I’ll leave it to the realm of personal advice, which I can’t supply.

As a matter of information, I’ll say the return of capital feature (that results in its tax treatment) results from MSTR’s having accumulated book losses resulting from its bitcoin holdings. For situations like this, I tend to worry about potential rule changes in the future, such as alternative minimum tax liability for unrealized bitcoin gains.

So in looking at the above table, assume, unless and until you determine otherwise based on your own situation, that the dividends are fully and immediately taxable as income.

Here, now, is a six-month performance comparison for the preferred issues. (It’s obviously not lengthy. But all four issues are relatively new. Still, we’re already seeing rational differences among the issues.

In addressing each issue individually, I won’t fuss about yield differences. All are quite good. And given how imprecise volatility assessments can be in the real word (as opposed to the classroom), I can easily envision yield differences being offset by differences in share price performance.

We see here that STRC has been top dog among the group.

STRK is the worst performer. But its convertibility feature means it has the greatest upside potential in the event bitcoin rallies.

STRF and STRD fall in the middle, and have essentially matched one another in terms of performance.

Rating For STRC — The So-Called Treasury Credit

The most salient aspect of STRC is its adjustable dividend.

STRC’s ideal price is $100. The company can’t really dictate that it actually trade at that level. But it can make a good effort,

MSTR targets a $99-$101 range for STRC. It determines price based on VWAP for the month.

(VWAP stands for Volume Weighted Average Price. Rather than an ordinary average with which we’re all familiar, the company uses an average in which the prices are weighted by the volume associated with each trade. Click here for more on VWAP, which is well recognized among serious traders.)

Here’s how each monthly STRC dividend is adjusted based on where VWAP came in.

This image shows that the STRC protocol has been working.

We see how the monthly dividend has been adjusted to successfully keep the price near $100. And note that this was during a time when bitcoin’s price fell.

And below, we see how the MSTR parent’s choice to boost its cash reserve has dramatically cut STRC’s observed volatility.

Here’s STRC’s price chart.

Notwithstanding a couple of noticeable dips, the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) is now above and rising relative to the 50-day EMA.

But I hesitate to use the word momentum here.

Look again at the chart, especially the price numbers running down the right-hand side of the image. Notice how narrow the tading range really is!

That comes from the way STRC was designed — a dividend that varies in order to keep pushing the price toward the $100 par value.

We’re looking here at stability by design.

Consider, too, Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

CMF tells us institutional buyers remain wary. CO tells us the general market has been back and forth.

It is still very early in STRC’s history. And STRC is set up such that it can’t rally much. It takes time to digest this sort of thing. And it might remain more an individual investor thing rather than an institutional vehicle. (The latter have other ways to derive income from bitcoin — e.g., derivatives).

STRC’s efforts to educate the investment world about STRC will likely help going forward.

I believe a growing track record will eventually make more investors more willing to own STRC. But even as things are now, the stock has been performing as designed.

I think STRC has a useful role in the investment world. It’s a high-yield security that’s quite different from the run-of-the-mill corporate junk bond.

Obviously, it’s harder for many to get comfortable with something related to bitcoin than even to a debt-heavy corporation.

But having done time in the 1980s as a junk bond mutual find manager, I don’t share that view.

It’s not like I’m diminishing the risks associated with crypto. Instead, I believe many high-yield bond investors underestimate the risks inherent in that universe. And high-yield bond ETFs have much lower yields. For example, the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) yields 5.75%. And the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) yields 6.55%.

There is, of course, a “risk” of a future bitcoin boom sharply boosting STRC sentiment, and its price. In that case, the STRC dividend will have to be cut to bring the price back toward its target.

But as the market becomes increasingly accustomed to how STRC works, I expect the price won’t likely rise so far as to precipitate a sharp lasting dividend cut.

All in all, STRC has a unique niche in the investment universe, And it’s one I like. Accordingly, I rate it “Buy.”

Rating For STRK — A Mellower Good-Yielding Version of the Parent

This has, during its market life thus far, shown itself to be exactly what it was designed to be. It’s a vehicle that, like MSTR, offers an amplified version of bitcoin returns. But in contrast to MSTR, the degree of amplification here is less stomach-churning. A nice yield is another plus. (MSTR doesn’t pay a common dividend.)

Notice below how effectively STRK’s convertibility feature softened bitcoin’s recent decline.

It appears, from the price chart, that Mr. Market appreciate what STRK offers.

I would not describe this as a bullish chart.

The 10-day EMA turned lower than did the 50-day measure. Meanwhile, CMF and CO are middling.

But consider the downturn bitcoin recently experienced (down about 40% in the last six months)! In this environment, you can’t expect to see a bullish chart for a security whose salient characteristic is bitcoin amplified — even if it does have a good yield.

Considering how difficult a time it’s lately been for bitcoin, this chart is probably as good (or less bad) than anyone has a right to expect.

STRK may be too risky for many (who don’t want any amplified version of bitcoin exposure). But for those whose risk tolerances encompass this sort of thing, I believe STRK nicely does the job. So…

For investors in this risk category, i rate STRK as a “Buy.”

Rating For STRF — Meh

I’d probably be willing to own STRF if neither STRC nor STRK existed.

It shares in STRC’s good income characteristics. And like STRK, it mutes the volatility of MSTR. Ultimately, though, STRC and STRK are better at performing their respective tasks.

STRC has more built-in stability due to the adjustable nature of its dividend. And STRK would better share in MSTR’s upside due to the STRK convertibility feature.

The price chart suggests the market sees STRF similarly.

Momentum, CMF and CO are all weak. Again, I (and Mr. Market) might see things differently if neither STRC nor STRK existed. But they both exist. And both, each in its own way, are better than STRF.

Note, too, that STRF pays a quarterly dividend. I prefer STRC’s monthly payout, which facilitates better compounding.

I therefore rate STRF as a “Sell.”

Rating For STRD — Just Saying No

This is the only non-cumulative preferred issue. And that, for me, is a deal breaker.

Normally, companies are free to skip paying preferred dividends. But if they do, they cannot resume paying in the future until after they have paid to shareholders all the arrears (i.e. making up for dividends that have been skipped).

Non-cumulative preferred is an exception. With these, skipped dividends are gone forever. The company can resume paying any time in the future without paying the arrears.

On the one hand, the non-cumulative feature makes it easier for a non-paying issuer to resume in the future. Some investors may see that as a positive.

I see the other side of this coin.

A company considering whether or not to skip a preferred dividend (which would likely tank the market prices of all its securities) might, if it believes a cash crunch to be temporary, be more incentivized to skip the non-cumulative issue.

Ultimately, though, given all investors must consider in deciding whether to own MSTR or any of its children, this is one question — risk factor — with which we can easily dispense.

The market seems less bothered by STRD’s being non-cumulative, as evidenced by STRD’s price chart.

Momentum, as illustrated by the relative positions and trends in the 10- and 50-day EMAs is lackluster, but not awful. Also both CMF and CO suggest currently are closer to neutrality than bearishness.

Oh well — Mr. Market and I will have to agree to disagree.

I see no reason to own STRD and rate it as a “Sell.”

SUMMARY

As to the four issues…

STRC, also known as “Stretch,” the so-called Treasury security with dividends periodically adjusted to push the issue’s price toward 100, is a “Buy.”

STRK, also known as “Strike,” the volatile convertible issue that like MSTR, is bitcoin amplified, is also a “Buy” given that it mutes a good deal of the volatility we see in the parent MSTR security.

STRF, also known as “Strife,” falling somewhere between STRC and STRK, seems to offer little appeal on its own, and therefore rates as a “Sell.”

STRD, also known as “Stride,” highly correlated with STRF but being non-cumulative, has no appeal in my view and therefore rates as a “Sell.

data.title: NN Group: Investment Case Remains Geared To Income Following Its 2025 Earnings
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, I was bullish on NN Group (NNGPF) (NNGRY) since mid-2024 due to an interesting combination of a high-dividend yield and relatively cheap valuation. However, following a strong share price return over the past couple of years, I have changed my recommendation to ‘hold’ on valuation grounds about two months ago. As the company has recently released its 2025 earnings, I think it’s now a good time to analyze its most recent financial performance and update its investment case, to see if its valuation remains fair or NN Group is now offering more value for long-term investors.

NN Group’s 2025 Earnings Analysis

NN Group reported a positive operating performance during 2025, with the company being able to achieve financial figures above its own expectations. Indeed, its organic capital generation (OCG) was around €2.1 billion, and its free cash flow amounted to €1.6 billion in the year, both being above the company’s own targets. Organic capital generation increased by 9% YoY, and was above NN Group’s target of €1.9 billion, supported by strong business momentum across its different units.

This strong capital and free cash flow generation was driven both by a combination of good top-line momentum and some cost initiatives, which resulted in improved business margins during the year.

Its value of new business (VNB) increased by 16% YoY for Insurance Europe and was up by 25% YoY in Japan, showing that its commercial momentum was strong as the company continues to push for growth on the distribution of capital-light products in the life segment.

This is positive both for the company’s revenues and earnings because these products don’t have investment guarantees and the investment risk is taken by the customers, being products that generate recurring fees for life insurance companies. This also means these products have higher margins for insurance companies and require less regulatory capital, being positive for cash flow generation and capital levels. Like many companies in the life insurance industry, NN Group has also pursued this strategy in recent years, and its business is gradually shifting from traditional toward unit-linked products in its domestic market and across other European countries, a strategy that has been an important contribution for higher capital generation and cash flows over the past few quarters.

As can be seen in the next graphs, while historically NN Group was a company with relatively low growth prospects, especially due to its high exposure to a mature insurance market like the Netherlands, its commercial strategy has allowed it to report high-single digit growth both for organic capital generation and free cash flows over the past four years which can be considered a positive outcome for a mature insurance company like NN Group.

This good performance was also supported by the Property & Casualty segment, that reported gross written premiums of €4.2 billion in 2025, up by 4% YoY, while the combined ratio of this unit was 92.9%, being well within its target range of 91-93% over the medium term. Its organic capital generation was €440 million in the P&C segment, well above its target of €400 million for 2025.

Its good performance across both the life and non-life segments justified its strong operational result growth, being up by 17% YoY to more than €3 billion in the year. Despite a higher operating result, negative revaluations regarding derivatives instruments and losses related to the sale of Turkey impacted its earnings, leading to a net income of €1.1 billion in 2025. This represented a decline of 25% YoY and showed a mixed picture regarding its earnings momentum, considering that from an operating performance NN Group reported a strong year, but its bottom-line was weak due to financial items and one-off impacts.

Regarding its profitability, its return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability in the insurance sector, was only 6.2% in 2025. This is at a very weak level and has been historically one of the main negative factors of NN Group’s investment case, largely justifying why its shares have traded consistently at a discounted valuation to its peers.

While NN Group has been successful in improving its operating results, capital and cash flow generation in recent years, its ROE ratio has remained at subpar levels, which is concerning regarding the structural profitability of its business model. This is explained by its large exposure to the Netherlands especially in the life segment, which is a business with low margins and relatively high capital requirements, being a structural headwind for profitability. Moreover, the company is quite conservative in its balance sheet management, maintaining an excess capital position and low financial leverage, being also factors contributing to low ROEs over the long term.

The company is making several efforts to improve its profitability through a different product mix, cost-cutting initiatives, and overall business growth, but according to analysts’ estimates its ROE is not expected to improve much over the coming years and remain in the high-single digits range over the next few years. This remains below average in the insurance sector, not being a great support for a higher valuation in the near future.

On the other hand, its good organic capital generation and balance sheet position are a strong support for higher capital returns to investors, which has been important for a re-rating of its shares in recent years. Indeed, one of its main business goals has been to deliver growing capital returns to shareholders, both through dividends and share buybacks. This has been supported by NN Group’s strong capital levels, as the Solvency II ratio has remained consistently above its target range of 150-200% over the medium term, showing that the company does not need to retain much earnings in its balance sheet. Indeed, at the end of 2025, its Solvency II ratio was 220% (vs. 194% in 2024), showing that NN Group has an excess capital position and can easily deliver a growing dividend to shareholders ahead.

As shown in the next graph, NN Group has a very good dividend growth history since its IPO in 2013 and is committed to maintain this trend in the near future, boding well for income-oriented investors. Related to 2025 earnings, its dividend per share was set at €3.88 per share, representing an increase of 13% YoY, and was above market’s expectations that were an annual dividend around €3.70 per share. At its current share price, this leads to a dividend yield around 5.7%, that is still attractive to income investors even though it’s lower than it was in the past as the company was yielding between 7-8% a couple of years ago.

Going forward, its dividend is expected to increase at high-single digits to a level around €4.31 per share by 2027, showing that NN Group is likely to maintain its growing dividend policy over the next few years.

On top of dividends, NN Group has also performed share buybacks consistently over the past decade, targeting at least about €300 million per year. Supported by strong results last year and higher capital generation than expected, the company has recently increased its share buyback program to €350 million annually, with further share repurchases expected ahead as the Solvency II capital ratio is well above 200% and the company can easily be more aggressive regarding its capital returns goals.

While higher capital returns are supportive for its share price over the coming months, on the other hand I see its current valuation as fair following a strong share price rally in recent years and a re-rating to much higher multiples than in the past. As can be seen in the next graph, NN Group is currently trading at about 0.86x book value, being the highest valuation level over the past three years and more than double its bottom valuation level back in 2023 (it was trading at about 0.4x book value at the time).

While NN Group has improved its business profile in recent years and has returned more capital to shareholders, justifying a higher valuation, I see its valuation now as fair considering that its ROE is in the high single digits and is not expected to become double-digit over the coming years. Therefore, a valuation below book value is warranted in my opinion and upside potential seems limited right now

Conclusion

NN Group has reported a positive operating performance lately and has increased its capital returns to shareholders supported by a strong capital position, but this seems to be priced in, and I see the company as fairly valued. Therefore, its investment case is more biased to income as NN Group still offers an interesting yield that seems to be sustainable over the medium-to-long term, even though on a total return basis prospects may be more muted in the near future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Federal Realty Investment Trust: A DC-Area Retail REIT With So Much Winning
data.description:
In the spirit of President's Day in the US this Monday Feb. 16th, a federal and market holiday, I chose to cover a REIT based in the Washington-DC metro area.

However, don't let the name fool you, since Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) is not leasing to the federal government, but is a retail REIT around since 1962 actually, that happens to be based in the DC suburbs of Maryland. For instance, their focus includes a portfolio of open-air shopping centers and mixed-use destinations.

In the wake of their recent earnings results on Feb. 12th, I am covering this REIT for the first time today, giving it a holistic review and opinion.

Thesis Summary

For my first coverage of this REIT, this stock wins with me for so many reasons. The macro environment/forecasts could support retail resilience and confidence in the near term, while this REIT continues growing and recycling its portfolio for the future, achieving impressive margins and FFO growth, an investment-grade credit rating from 2 agencies, ample dividend coverage with proven growth, and technical chart indicator pointing to a bullish case.

The following worksheet summarizes what drove my score.

Continue reading further to learn more about how each of these categories contributed to the overall rating score holistically.

Macro & Sector Outlook

To understand what could impact the niche of retail REITs, let's consider the types of properties this REIT invests in, from their web portfolio, which shows diversified retail properties across the US and with a variety of tenants including some major grocery and retail brands:

What first comes to mind, therefore, is consumer confidence and recession outlooks, and this is highly impactful for the discretionary retail spending categories, since it can financially impact retail tenants this REIT leases to.

Colliers' 2026 Retail Outlook, published in January, pointed out some positive tailwind, saying "more than one-third of consumers (34.4%) plan to spend less in 2026, yet a majority (65.6%) expect to maintain or increase spending."

As far as retailers' demand for space, the same study said "retail demand is projected to remain positive through 2026, supported by healthy leasing velocity and limited new supply."

I think these are two points that can be key upside drivers for this niche, in addition to Business Insider reporting just days ago that Ed Yardeni, founder of Yardeni Research, "re-upped his bullish thesis, noting that he sees no reason for the US to tumble into a recession in the coming four years."

Given the multiple potential upside drivers mentioned, I gave the stock a bullish view in this section.

Competitive Positioning

The following could be considered 3 good retail REITS to use as peer comparisons: Realty Income (O), Kimco Realty (KIM), and Regency Centers (REG).

The market cap range of this peer group is a bit wide, from $9B - $60B, with FRT actually being the smallest of the group with a market cap of just around $9B, so that is something to consider.

Peer data shows that FRT had the lowest 5-year revenue CAGR of this peer group at just +9.1%, however it leads the group in return on equity /ROE of just over +12%, which is very impressive.

However, where FRT lagged was in 10 year price return vs the S&P500, a major benchmark to compare against, as shown in momentum data. While no one else in this peer group beat the benchmark, they all had a better return than FRT, indicating that some peers may be prone to stronger market momentum over time.

Where I think FRT could have competitive positioning among retail REITS is that it has diversified beyond purely retail, and has grown to include mixed-use property that includes residential and office. We can visualize that from their Q3 investor deck (pg 8):

So, considering the portfolio diversification, and beating the peer group on ROE, but also considering weaker revenue CAGR and price return vs peers, I gave the stock a neutral view in this section.
