Company Growth Factors

Having covered some REITs that lease to the federal government as a tenant, such as Easterly Govt Properties, the growth in such tends to be impacted by federal budget decisions and agency needs. In this case of this retail REIT, company-specific growth factors are impacted by the company's portfolio growth or recycling strategy, and growing leases and high occupancy levels which drive future rental income.

Notable to mention from the very recent Q4 and FY25 results are that the REIT announced 2025 was a record-breaking year for leasing:

Achieved an all-time company record total leasing volume of 2.5 million square feet of retail space.

Further, they mentioned occupancy levels well above +90%, saying "the comparable portfolio occupancy was 94.5%, up 40 basis points sequentially and up 50 basis points year-over-year."

These, I think, are two materially relevant growth levers to consider that can drive upside.

In terms of portfolio growth, keep in mind this REIT has been recycling properties and so it both acquires and disposes of properties, and also redevelops some. For instance, it announced in Q4 results that its redevelopment project in Pennsylvania "will bring over 260 residential units, 52,000 square feet of retail space, and a 438-stall parking garage."

REITS may recycle properties for different reasons, such as disposing of some to raise cash and acquire others that could generate a much better return.

If we go back to their Q3 investor deck (pg 15), there is a great visualization showing that even though they have been disposing properties, they also are actively acquiring others, which I think could drive upside to the portfolio if they can achieve better returns this way.

Complimenting this positive view of mine is Seeking Alpha data showing expected fwd revenue growth of +4.9%, beating its overall sector average.

So, the evidence here is overwhelmingly in favor of being bullish in this section.

Profitability Metrics

Next, let's consider some metrics related to profitability. The margin comparison of choice for me is the EBITDA margin, since in REITS the net profit margin could be greatly distorted by depreciation.

Here is how the EBITDA margin compares in this peer group:

The good news for FRT in this case is that it is 2nd in this peer group with an EBITDA margin of around +65%, beaten only by Realty Income. These two stand out for me for the best earnings efficiency from the core business, ignoring building depreciation.

Another strong point for FRT, according to Seeking Alpha data, is a 5-year FFO growth CAGR of +9.8%, beating the sector average, which is impressive. This is a critical metric in the world of equity REITs.

Company guidance on core FFO growth is also positive. The recent Q4 commentary shows the company's confidence about FY26 FFO per share, expecting $7.42 - $7.52, vs the FY25 figure of $7.22 we see on the most recent income statement data.

The analyst consensus also calls for modest FFO growth of 3 - 4% roughly in FY26 and FY27.

Looking beyond just these figures, think about the actual business of an equity REIT and what the key driver of expenses are. Unless it is a net-lease REIT, a key cost driver could be rising property expenses. This REIT has managed to keep property expense growth in line with rental income growth. Consider from income statement data that YoY (FY25 vs FY24) rental income growth was around 6 - 7%, and property expense growth also was around 6 -7 % too and not much higher than rental income growth percentages:

Given these multiple points of evidence I presented, I am actually highly bullish in this section.

Balance Sheet Risk

Moving on, here are some material points about balance sheet risk you should know.

The first thing that got my attention is that this REIT got an investment-grade rating not from one but two major agencies, a BBB+ from S&P and a Baa1 from Moody's.

However, one area that moves the risk lever somewhat higher is data showing FRT's debt-to-equity at 1.63, the highest of this peer group, and on a rising trend it seems, indicating greater reliance on leverage in relation to equity:

Now, use of leverage is commonplace among REITs, but it is worth considering how much the D/E deviates from similar peers, and here it is creeping upward.

On the asset side, since this REIT's major asset is not a liquid one like cash or tradeable securities but an illiquid asset like real estate, as shown in balance sheet data, we should consider a common concern in equity REITS and that is over-exposure to a single tenant, since that tenant running into financial troubles could also spell trouble for the REIT.

If we turn back to the Q3 investor deck which is not all that long ago, we see the top 10 tenants make up less than 15% of this REIT's ABR (annualized base rent), with the top 2 tenants, retail brand TJX and grocery store owner Ahold driving less than 5% of ABR:

This indicates lack of tenant over-exposure and so lowers the risk needle.

Although as of the writing of this article I did not see the company posted their Q4 investor deck yet, subsequent to Q3 results we can see the company posted their end-of-quarter and end-of-year supplemental (pg 29), showing a similar scenario to the Q3 investor deck, but also considerable top-10 exposure to consumer essentials like groceries/pharmacy (Ahold, CVS, Whole Foods, Albertsons):

Thus, this REIT seems to be a case of high leverage but also a strong credit rating and low tenant over-concentration, along with exposure to some big name tenants across consumer essentials. For this reason, I went with a neutral view.

Dividend Outlook

As we approach the final stretch of this article, considering that dividends are a major selling point for investing in REITs, let's talk about them briefly.

Dividend yield is modest around 4.3%, mostly in line with the sector average.

The greater strength is proven, steady dividend growth over 10 years, with a 10 year CAGR of +2.11%, beating the sector average. I would point out a sign of business resilience in the following chart from Seeking Alpha, showing that even though this is a retail REIT it did not seem to cut its dividend during the pandemic years of 2020-2021, a period that I would call a major market shock to the retail sector:

To get a sense of dividend coverage ability, the metric that matters is the AFFO per share. We can see from income statement data over the last year the AFFO/share was between $1.70 - $1.90, while the most recent quarterly dividend was $1.13, indicating this REIT can cover its dividend from AFFO and not have to borrow cash to do so. This implies a payout ratio around the 60% range, which I think leaves ample cash wiggle room.

The positive FFO growth forecast I mentioned earlier also can add confidence to the potential for further dividend growth.

In fact, it seems this REIT's history has stressed dividend growth, and the company had boasted in its Q3 investor deck (pg 28) that it was the only REIT recognized as a "Dividend King."

So, the dividend story is that the yield is in line with the sector, the growth case is proven and strong and showed resilience through market shocks, and the AFFO coverage is ample. For these overwhelming reasons, I am highly bullish in this section as well.

Valuation Metrics

Next, I made the following worksheet comparing valuation vs peers, and determining a share price forecast using some of that valuation data, which uses Seeking Alpha data:

In the worksheet, FRT is only slightly undervalued vs the peer avg when it comes to the forward price/FFO multiple, indicating perhaps the market expects its future earnings to perform relatively close to this peer group. Its trailing price/book value is somewhat overvalued to the peer avg, which could indicate the market viewing its portfolio as very high quality.

However, my price forecast implies just a +4% upside above the most recent share price (Sunday night's price) so that hurts the bullish case in this section, despite the confidence on future earnings and high quality portfolio perception. So, I have to be neutral here.

Technical Charting

In this last topic, I move away from fundamental topics an consider the technical chart below, which compares the share price movement to the 50-day and 200-day simple moving averages, over the last 1 year period:

In the chart, we can spot a golden cross formation (buy signal) in the autumn when the 50-day SMA crossed above the 200-day. The two moving averages continued to run parallel but also point upward. In addition, the share price has remained above the short-term moving average which acts as support.

In this scenario, I am more inclined to say bullish than hold, and the giveaway is the share price keeping its bullish momentum nicely.

While it does not always occur, in this case the technical pattern supports the otherwise predominantly bullish view of the rest of the article and affirms the final score as a buy.

Risks and Challenge to Thesis

A challenge to my bullish view could come from something I have not included in this discussion yet on purpose, and that is the role of interest rate risk in the REIT sector overall. This REIT, who already has an elevated D/E, obviously relies heavily on leverage already, and has shown portfolio growth ambitions and development/redevelopment plans, and so cost of capital plays a huge role here.

If interest rates stay higher for longer, that could make my bullish case seem overly confident.

Although Fed decisions impact short-term funding, it could eventually spill into long-term debt costs, so as a baseline let's consider CME Fedwatch shows an overwhelming probability the Fed will not cut rates further at its March meeting, and low probability at its April meeting as well.

Now, such decisions are also impacted by inflation levels and economic conditions/employment, etc., so it can be an ever-changing risk this sector can be expected to face in both the near and long term.

Conclusion

To wrap up this President's Day article, my buy rating actually agrees this time with the entire consensus as of Monday (Wall St, SA analysts, SA quant system) which is also a rare event.

I've shown not only why this REIT has several growth levers but also why it can support its dividend which it has grown nicely, and it makes my list of dividend ideas for the month as a President's Day treat from this author!

As a REIT investor myself, I would include this on a watchlist of mixed retail REITs with a solid dividend income story but also capital growth potential too, and opportunity to gain portfolio exposure to the diversified retail REIT niche as part of a larger diversified REIT portfolio.

data.title: PayPal Is Mispriced And The Market Is Overlooking The Fundamentals
data.description:
PayPal Holdings (PYPL) has been one of the worst stocks in the market. Over the past month, shares have declined by -28.85%, over the past year, they have declined by -47.40%, and roughly -86.94% since shares exceeded $300 in 2021. PYPL has earned the nickname Painpal for a reason, and no matter what they have done, from buying back a massive amount of shares to changing CEOs, nothing has added shareholder value. PYPL is now trading at the same level it did in April of 2017 despite deploying $36.22 billion in buybacks since the beginning of 2017 and reducing the number of shares outstanding by more than 23%. Long-term shareholders have a legitimate gripe considering management repurchased $2.16 billion worth of shares in 2020 when the share price was rising and $4.4 billion worth of shares when PYPL was trading at its all-time highs. The market punished PYPL once again after they reported Q4 earnings when their revenue missed expectations by $111.49 million and guidance that was anything but bullish. PYPL now trades at 7.54 times earnings, and I believe the vultures are going to come out and pick away at this investment. PYPL has the image of a carcass in the eyes of the market, but the revenue continues to grow YoY, and the free cash flow (FCF) is still a staggering amount for a company whose market cap is $37.09 billion. I'm still bullish on PYPL, as this is a broken stock, not a broken company. The chart makes it look like PYPL is going under, but after going through the financials, that couldn't be further from the truth.

Following up on my previous article

At the beginning of August, I had written an article about PayPal after Q2 earnings discussing why PYPL was one of my largest contrarian ideas. PYPL remained very profitable as it continued to grow its revenue, buyback shares, and introduce products such as PayPal World. PYPL traded at a discount compared to its peers despite best efforts to change the market's perception. This has been a horrible investment, and I have gotten this wrong for some time now. I am following up with a new article to dissect the numbers and lay out my case as to why there is still value to be unlocked. PYPL has never seen a year of deceleration in its revenue, and its printing FCF while buying back massive amounts of shares. After running the numbers again, I think that PYPL is mispriced and there is a lot of upside from these levels in the future.

PYPL is drastically undervalued

If you look at the chart I created below and take the name PYPL, this would look like a healthy company. Over the past 9 years since the 2016 fiscal year ended, the revenue has increased by $22.33 billion (205.96%). There has not been a single year where the revenue hasn't appreciated on a YoY basis by at least $1 billion. The gross profit has increased by $8.6 billion (167.67%) over this period, and there has only been 1 year in the past decade where the gross profit margin slipped under 40%. The amount of EBITDA and net income have both increased by 216.32% and 273.52% since the close of 2016. Investors are paying the same price for PYPL as they were in the first half of 2017 before all this growth happened. If the revenue and profitability were declining, I would understand that. Maybe PYPL didn't deserve to exceed a $300 share price in 2021, but trading at the same level as it did in the 1st half of 2017 seems very overdone.

I think that PYPL can exceed their 2026 guidance due to the amount of shares their $6 billion of buybacks can repurchase at these levels. There is a footnote on EPS that PYPL benefited from a $0.14 positive impact in 2025 from their investment portfolio. In 2025, PYPL generated $5.46 of EPS. When the $0.14 is stripped away, it puts them at $5.32 of EPS, which is a decline of -2.56%. According to the 10-K, PYPL had a weighted average number of shares of 959 million when they determined their basic EPS of $5.46. According to the guidance in PYPL's shareholder deck, they will repurchase $6 billion worth of shares in 2026. Hypothetically, if they pay an average of $50 per share, they will repurchase 120 million shares, which is 12.5% of the shares they used in the year-end EPS calculations. If PYPL is able to pay an average of $47 per share, they would repurchase 127.66 million shares, which is 13.31% of the shares used in the calculation.

The consensus estimate for PYPL in 2026 is that they will generate $34.18 billion in revenue, which is 3.04% YoY growth, as they would add over $1 billion to the top line while producing $5.34 in EPS. Based on the current share price, the chart, and the amount of capital being allocated to buybacks, I believe that PYPL can repurchase between 10-15% of their shares this year, which would offset the $0.14 of EPS gained in the investment portfolio during 2025 and allow them to exceed the consensus estimates on EPS for 2026. I think that the market is discounting how many shares PYPL can buy back at these levels and the ramifications it would have on their 2026 and 2027 EPS.

PYPL is being valued as if it's going under at these levels, yet the analyst community sees forward EPS growth over the next several years. PYPL has a gross profit margin of 41.48%, an EBITDA margin of 20.04%, an FCF margin of 16.77%, and a net income margin of 15.78%. PYPL is trading at less than 6 times EBITDA, operating income, and cash from operations. It's also trading at less than 7 times FCF and 8 times net income. The analyst community consisting of 40 for 2027 and 17 for 2028, is projecting that PYPL will generate $5.82 of EPS in 2027 and $6.45 in 2028. If PYPL hits those targets that means that it's trading at 7.54 times 2026 earnings, 6.92 times 2027 earnings, and 6.92 times 2028 earnings. From a profitability standpoint, PYPL makes zero sense, but what's even more interesting is that the analyst community believes that PYPL will grow EPS by over 20% over the next 2 years. When taking the name out of it, this doesn't seem like a company that is on the verge of disappearing, considering revenue has never declined YoY and there is still substantial EPS growth on the horizon. I think that the market is overreacting to questions that can't be proven or disproven, and PYPL is too cheap at these levels.

PayPal still has a thriving business

2025 turned 2025 into a year that its earnings are becoming more diversified, durable, and predictable. PYPL grew its total payment volume (TPV) 9% to $475.1 billion in Q4 and delivered $1.53 of EPS, which was up 38% YoY in Q4. For the 2025 fiscal year, TPV reached $1.79 trillion with operating margins expanding and large amounts of FCF. I think the big aspect that is being overlooked is the fact that PYPL is less dependent on any single product cycle as Venmo is scaling into a monetized commerce platform, omni is building everyday spending behavior, and enterprise payments is improving economics through price-to-value discipline and paid value-added services. This allowed PYPL to generate $8.68 billion in revenue for Q4 which was up 4% YoY/ PYPL's operating income increased 5% to $1.51 billion, while its operating margin increased 19 bps to 17.4%. PYPL's cash generation is the foundation of the bull thesis, as they generated $6.4 billion in adjusted FCF, which is a key measure to judge them by because it strips away timing impacts tied to BNPL receivable sales.

Part of the bear thesis is that transactions are shrinking, but I believe the correct framing of this idea is that PYPL is actively reshaping lower-quality volume in payment service provider (PSP) and unbranded processing while driving healthier engagement elsewhere. Yes, total payment transactions declined 4% to 25.4 billion in 2025, but transactions excluding PSP increased 6% to 16.1 billion, and the TTM total payment activity without PSP increased 5%. PYPL's 2025 results disprove the bear thesis as revenue increased 4% YoY and their transaction margin grew 6% YoY. There is more activity flowing through PYPL's system as TPV increased 7% YoY, which is a critical reason why there is still value to be unlocked. If people and enterprises weren't using PYPL and its products, then these metrics would be declining, but they aren't.

The analyst community expects PYPL to grow, and the underlying metrics support that idea. Venmo reached 100 million accounts, and there are 67 million active monthly accounts within its ecosystem, which is up 7% YoY. Venmo's revenue increased 20% to $1.7 billion in 2025. As more consumers use Venmo for everyday commerce, the TPV and revenue should continue to grow, which is the opposite of what a dead company does. The PSP business has historically been viewed as lower-margin and competitively priced, but in 2025, PYPL showed that it can scale and expand margins. Enterprise payments delivered 7 consecutive quarters of profitable growth and returned to double-digit volume growth in Q4, which indicates that the addressable market is expanding.

Ace in the hole nobody is thinking about

PYPL just appointed Enrique Lores as their new CEO after he served as CEO of HP Inc. (HPQ) for 6 years. He led HP through a strategic transition and expanded the business beyond PCs and printers. He has been brought in to add shareholder value, which is something that PYPL has been unable to do for several years. When you look at PYPL's balance sheet, they have $10.42 billion in cash and short-term investments on hand with an additional $4.33 billion in long-term investments on the balance sheet. They only have $9.99 billion in long-term debt, costing them $441 million in annualized interest expenses. PYPL also has 23,800 employees. PYPL can conduct a reduction in force (RIF) to increase margins and allocate an additional $5 billion in buybacks from the balance sheet without sacrificing their financial security. At the current market cap of $37.09 billion, PYPL can repurchase 29.66% of the shares outstanding from the $6 billion of planned buybacks and using $5 billion off the balance sheet. This could send shares significantly higher, and I wouldn't be surprised if something like this occurs.

Risks to investing in PayPal

PYPL has been a horrible investment for several years as shares have declined more than -80% from their highs. The market's perception of PYPL is dismal, and nothing has been able to change this. The market didn't like PYPL's guidance, and shares fell off a cliff just when many investors thought that a bottom was in. PYPL is an uphill battle and I believe that there is still a lot of risk in the name. The momentum is to the downside, and while the fundamentals tell a different story, there is minimal confidence in PYPL. In addition to sentiment, there are operational risks, as PYPL's payment transactions and transactions per account declined YoY. This could continue to decline and cause the narrative around minimal growth turning to negative growth to intensify. Investors should do their own due diligence, as PYPL is not an easy stock to own, as it's sat out of the bull market and caused losses for many shareholders.

Conclusion

I have been wrong on PYPL, and the sentiment is not bad; it's horrible, but I am still taking a contrarian approach. PYPL looks more like a broken stock, not a broken company. The revenue continues to increase; PYPL trades at less than 6 times EBITDA, operating income, and cash from operations. The analyst community is projecting both revenue and EPS growth over the next several years, and PYPL is trading at 7.45 times 2026 earnings. Shares of PYPL can continue to go lower, but I don't believe they can go much lower as they are trading at 6.67 times FCF and 7.09 times net income. I think that there is a huge opportunity in PYPL because there is a tremendous amount of FCF and cash on the balance sheet to buyback shares with, and if they implement a RIF, then margins should expand. I think there are levers to pull, and I can see PYPL significantly higher later in 2026.

data.title: Credo: Ramping Into An AI Super-Cycle (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

Credo Technology Group Holding (CRDO) is focusing on Active Electrical Cable (AEC) products that basically manage data traffic at incredible speeds (up to 1.6 Terabits per second). Their AECs are in high demand from data centers and hyperspaces that require a lot of compute power to train LLMs and run AI-driven apps (and not just AI-driven). In addition to AECs, Credo building a sophisticated platform that also offer advanced signal-processing chips, optical solutions, and memory tools to ensure data moves smoothly across even the largest supercomputer clusters of their customers.

The demand boost that CRDO has gone through over the past few quarters, and the momentum levels the stock price has managed to achieve thanks to that fundamental driver, is only gaining heat lately. From what I can see, Credo's connectivity solutions look much more reliable and energy efficient for its customers compared to anything else on the end market. In 2H 2026, Credo should get a material revenue boost for their new Blue Heron 224G AI scale-up retimer, and the second half of 2026 has already started (Credo reports for its fiscal Q3 2026 in about 2 weeks from now). I'm not expecting anything extraordinary in Q3 compared to what's already reflected in the consensus estimates, but I think management will upgrade its Q4 2026 guidance, noting extreme demand for AEC solutions in general. And it seems very likely to me that management will keep its narrative around Blue Heron positive, pointing to bullish momentum into FY2027. Also, the previous consensus beating history looks interesting - it's an indirect sign that the current consensus may be wrong again.

I think that as Credo is continuously ramping into the current AI super cycle with its top-notch AEC offerings, the business is likely to flourish in the next 2-3 years at least. And it makes the stock price relatively cheap already today.

Record-Breaking Q2 Results And What's In Store For Q3

Credo's fiscal Q2 2026 revenue amounted to ~$268 million, and it was a massive 272.1% YoY rise over Q2 2025, and it was more than 20% above Q12026. Seeking Alpha's data shows that Credo beat the consensus estimate by over 14%, although it looks like the initial consensus wasn't set too low for beating. In terms of non-GAAP EPS, we saw $0.67, which was 35%+ above the consensus - for the 5th time in a row, CRDO beat the bottom line consensus by >25%.

The above strength can be explained by Credo's profitability improvement - its adjusted gross margin amounted to 66.7%, and it's 4.1% above last year's metric, hence the difference to consensus. Despite rising so quickly, and expanding operations on different verticals, Credo had positive unlevered FCF of ~$56.2 million on a TTM basis, according to Seeking Alpha, because even the rise in CAPEX couldn't offset the CFO generation. In other words, Credo is getting extremely profitable in real time, and what it's spending on expansion seems to be leading to almost immediate accretive effect.

It's mu first research article on CRDO, and based on multiple other pieces on the stock, I know that the firm used to suffer from the lack of customer diversification. It didn't suffer in terms of financials - there was just a constant Damocles sword hanging above that made Credo an overall risky investment. But the tide on that front is finally turning with Credo signing its 5th hyperscaler recently - that customer has begun contributing initial revenue, and given the volumes of the other 4 large customers, I think that there's a chance that we'll see abnormal contribution from the 5th one already in Q3. The management has been quite bullish when it comes to the medium-term prospects, thus indirectly making my conviction stronger. Ahead of the upcoming Q3 release, the management decided to issue a preliminary upgrade a few days ago, raising the revenue range to $404-408 million from the initial revenue guidance of $335-345 million. The effective difference is at 19.4%, which is truly a lot.

One might question, "Why did they issue that upgrade - why not just wait for the earnings date and surprise the market in a positive way?"

I don't have the ultimate right answer for that question, but I think it's logical that they came out with an update, letting the Wall Street analysts adjust their views ahead of the date and not lead to excessive market volatility right after the print. It doesn't make the management team look great when they can't come up with a figure that actually predicts the business growth, and it works both ways.

What really matters when such updates take place is the market's reaction. On the surface, we see that while CRDO initially went up over 10%, its 1‑month correction still looks horrible (-17.33%, based on YCharts data):

And if we go deeper and take a look at how the analysts' consensus changed since the update, we'll see that the changes look minimal - yes, there was a jump of over 12% over the past 30 days when it comes to the Q3 revenue consensus, but the figure itself is still almost 5% below the new midpoint.

In Q2, when the revenue soared by >272%, the EPS went up by 857.14% YoY, so the operating leverage was massive, with the bottom line out-rising revenue by a factor of 3.15. I don't expect CRDO to keep the same level of operating leverage for Q3 because management initially guided for a slight GP margin contraction on a QoQ basis (from over 67% down to the range of 64% to 66%). But even at a 65% margin, we're likely to see a much more pronounced EPS growth than the revenue growth implied by the midpoint of the new guidance. In other words, I believe we might see Credo's EPS rising by 350-400% YoY. If so, the actual Q3 EPS should come in at $1.1875 vs. the current consensus of $0.94:

If Credo reaches that level of EPS for the quarter, management is likely to update the Q4 guidance as well, in turn leading to another round of bullish revisions from analysts. It's a vicious circle where one positive leads to another, especially when the environment remains in favor of this momentum.

Credo's Valuation Explained

The above dynamics, if they go according (or close) to the plan I've drawn, make CRDO look like a bargain in today's market. Why? Well, as I'm writing these lines, the stock is valued at less than 29x FY2027 earnings. Recall that Credo is about to report its fiscal Q3 2026, so its financial year is a bit shifted, and FY2027 comes earlier than for most public companies out there. So, if my assumption of $1.1875 in Q3 EPS comes true (or we see something close to that figure), and the market starts to reprice the FY2027 EPS, we should logically see CRDO's forward multiples dropping like rocks.

If Credo's FY2027 doubles versus FY2026 - and it won't be an excessively bullish assumption when we look at what CRDO's EPS figures did in 2025-2026 - we'll see $6.22 instead of the consensus $4.31. That would make CRDO trade at ~20x forward earnings amid 100% rise in earnings. It's basically a forward PEG of ~0.20x.

Assuming that Credo trades at 30x FY2027 earnings, having the EPS figure I'm targeting ($6.22, not $4.31), the stock turns out to be 49-50% undervalued in the medium term. So, I don't view CRDO as an overpriced semis stock like many other names in its niche these days.

Risks To My Bullish Views

My main thesis on Credo is based on multiple bullish assumptions that might fail to materialize in reality. Credo is still heavily reliant on just a couple of its key customers, so whenever one of them decides to leave for an alternative AEC solution, Credo's top line will drop. I see no way the management could save the situation if that happens.

I also make really bold assumptions regarding the Q3 EPS figure, extrapolating the operating leverage of Q2 with a discount. That doesn't look reliable, but it's the only way we can somehow predict what's coming for the quarter. The actual figure is definitely going to be different. If the difference turns out to be material, and the margin contraction that management guided for is heavier than I thought, both my Q3 and FY2027 EPS figures will be off by a mile. Credo might lose the reasons for having a premium valuation in that case (when the business growth is insufficient).

Conclusion

Credo looks like an ideal candidate to buy ahead of its upcoming earnings report release (set for after-market, 3/2/2026) because the recent consolidation has already shaken out "weak hands," while the business has only gotten stronger - as evidenced by management's update ahead of the print.

In my opinion, Credo Technology might add another 50% to its market capitalization over the next 12-24 months if it executes at least partially on the targets I've laid out in my article. So far, the level of execution has been great, judging by the recent past. I think the stock deserves a "Buy."
